one_liner: Every misspelling of Heineken becomes a real bottle.
search_doc:
Every misspelling of Heineken becomes a real bottle.
Mechanisms: brand-name-recontextualisation, limited-edition-product-as-media, anniversary-as-anti-celebration, user-generated-language
Idea: To celebrate Heineken's 150th anniversary, the brand embraced the way drinkers actually call it: 'Whateverken'. Bottles are relabelled with mispronunciations collected from around the world, so that people could enjoy a Heineken in their own way.
Insight: Consumers mispronounce Heineken constantly — instead of correcting them, the brand could turn every misspelling into an act of ownership.

Bottle line-up showing the misspelling press
Contextual outdoor and ambient executions

inferred.insight: Consumers mispronounce Heineken constantly — instead of correcting them, the brand could turn every misspelling into an act of ownership.
inferred.one_liner: Every misspelling of Heineken becomes a real bottle.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Anti-anniversary platform that converts consumer mispronunciations into 2 billion limited-edition bottles, delivering +32% sales lift and +354% brand recall on the brand's most successful global PR launch.

visual.style_description: A giant globe constructed from Heineken bottles dominates the centre, showing customers holding the mislabelled 'Whateverken' bottles around it; press logo strip across the top, dense metric and quote blocks on the right, with a small green-bottle line-up at the bottom left.

idea.rationale: Refusing to celebrate a 150-year anniversary and instead celebrating consumers' mispronunciations is a sharp inversion of the milestone-ad cliche, and the 'Whateverken' coinage instantly turns a product flaw (a hard-to-spell brand name) into ownable brand equity. The mechanism is genuinely original within the global beer category and lands on a single, memorable artefact: a mislabelled bottle the drinker recognises as their version.

strategy.rationale: Heineken is the Dutch global lager brand whose long-running 'open your world' platform celebrates cosmopolitan consumption, so embracing how the world actually pronounces the name is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The stated context — Heineken as the most misspelled beer brand in the world — is a plausible cultural observation, but the board does not source the claim or quantify the mispronunciation rate, leaving the founding insight as a soft point.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is product-first: mislabelled limited-edition bottles become the core media unit, with contextual outdoor, ambient and a global earned-PR loop layered on top. Distribution scale (153 countries) and the bottle-line-up press image carry the platform across markets at the same time, which is the right channel mix for an FMCG anniversary moment built around a physical SKU.

impact.rationale: The +32% beer sales figure is the right business outcome for a beer brand and is the strongest claim on the board, though the base period and geographic scope are not disclosed. The +354% brand recall sits unanchored without a baseline, and '2 billion bottles in 153 countries' reads as a production figure rather than a counted consumer response; the 'most successful global PR launch' is an entrant claim. Press coverage across The Sun, FT, Vogue and Adweek is credible breadth, though impressions and audited reach are not shown.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the anti-anniversary mechanic turns the brand's most-mispronounced status into a globally manufactured product, delivering a counted sales lift on top of breadth of earned coverage.

one_liner: The pill imprint IS the phone number to quit.
search_doc:
The pill imprint IS the phone number to quit.
Mechanisms: everyday-object-transformation, branded-utility-as-response, data-as-content, behaviour-change-call-to-action
Idea: 855-HOW-TO-QUIT. A helpline that reaches people in the most critical moment — when they have a pill in their hand. It turns the mandatory imprint codes on opioid pills into phone extensions: codeine (IP33) is dialed as 4733, oxycodone (K9) as 59. The caller then hears the story of someone who managed to quit that very pill, followed by live advice from experts.
Insight: Every opioid pill is already engraved with a unique identifier — the imprint code — which can be turned into the phone number that connects the user to someone who quit the same pill.

idea.rationale: Mapping the mandatory imprint codes on opioid pills to phone extensions on a 855-HOW-TO-QUIT helpline — so the object of addiction becomes the way out — is a true everyday-object-transformation with an unambiguous response (dial the number on the pill). The pill-as-CTA inversion is original and category-precise.

strategy.rationale: Anzen Health is a US healthcare consultancy positioning itself around addiction-recovery support, so building a helpline mechanism for opioid users is mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. Context is anchored with concrete, citable US figures ('one death every 6 minutes', 6.1M Americans with opioid-use disorder), and pill imprint codes are a real FDA-mandated feature — the insight is verifiable rather than asserted; soft spot is that 'Anzen' is a low-recognition brand and the board does not explain how callers reach Anzen-branded services after dialling.

execution.rationale: The response architecture maps pill-imprint codes to phone extensions, with a story from someone who quit that exact pill followed by live expert callbacks — the response asset is well-engineered for the moment-of-temptation. Contextual OOH in pharmacies and public spaces plus social/film and NGO/artist/media coalition backing give the launch reach, though the actual orchestration of how each pill imprint becomes discoverable to the user at the critical moment relies heavily on the OOH and not on the pill packaging itself.

impact.rationale: 8,000+ calls in four weeks and 142 recovery stories volunteered are absolute counted responses, but the volume is small relative to a US population of 6.1M opioid sufferers, and the headline '137 million contacts' is an entrant-reported reach claim, not a response. The press strip across Bloomberg, AP, CBS, Forbes, USA Today and Reuters is a credible signal of earned conversation but does not substitute for behaviour-change data (continuation in treatment, relapse outcomes), which the board does not disclose.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the pill-imprint-as-helpline-extension is a genuinely original everyday-object-transformation, but the response volume (8,000 calls) is modest against a 6.1M-sufferer population and the deeper behaviour-change outcomes are absent.

idea.rationale: Recasting every Pedigree media impression as a response-driving ad for a specific local shelter dog is a category-redefining response mechanism: each placement is itself the call-to-action, with a QR handoff to that named dog's profile. The reframe turns media spend into adoption inventory rather than awareness, which is rare for a CPG.

strategy.rationale: Pedigree is Mars's global dog-food brand whose long-running 'Dogs Rule' / shelter-adoption advocacy has anchored its purpose for two decades, so converting paid media into an adoption engine is exact mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The stated insight — shelter dogs are overlooked because their photos can't match brand-grade imagery — is an observable cultural truth the AI 'glow-up' attacks directly; it is asserted as self-evident rather than backed with adoption-rate data on the board, the only soft spot.

execution.rationale: The bespoke AI-restoration pipeline lets a single shelter snapshot drop into any Pedigree creative, delivered as geo-targeted DOOH near each dog's actual shelter with QR-to-adoption profile and an auto-removal handoff when a dog is adopted. The orchestration closes the response loop from billboard impression to shelter visit to adoption, with a yellow-poster template keeping coherence across hundreds of unique dogs.

impact.rationale: The headline behaviour-change number — 50% of featured dogs adopted within two weeks — is the right business outcome for an adoption campaign and a strong response proof, though the cohort size isn't disclosed so '50% of how many' remains open. The 6x lift in shelter site visits is a credible directional proxy; the '100% purpose-led media investment by 2026' is a commitment rather than a result but signals durable footprint.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the mechanism turns Pedigree's entire media buy into a response product — each impression is a working adoption ad for a specific, local dog — with counted adoptions as the proof point.

The mechanic is simple and recognisable rather than category-defining, but it's a clear receipt-as-coupon with an unambiguous call-to-action.

The context — Dubai has the most fine-dining restaurants per capita in the world — is plausible but not data-backed on the board (no source cited). Audience targeting is sharp: high-end-restaurant diners in a specific city, reached via geo-targeted ads triggered by venue exit.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs through geo-fenced mobile ads that fire when diners leave fine-dining restaurants, prompting them to bring their bill to McDonald's for a free meal, supported by OOH, digital and social amplification. Channel choice is fit for the audience (mobile geo-targeting at the right physical moment), but the response asset itself is a familiar coupon mechanic rather than a structurally novel device.

impact.rationale: The +43% footfall and +35% related-sales numbers are unbased — '43% of what baseline footfall, across how many stores and what period?' is the open question. The 62M reach is entrant-reported and is a surrogate rather than a Direct-response count, and +15% brand love is a tracker metric without a stated cohort. Press breadth across Forbes, AdAge, Adweek and Contagious is a credible earned-conversation signal but does not substitute for an anchored response number.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the receipt-for-burger swap is a clean local activation with the right geo-targeted channel, but headline lifts ride on unbased percentages and the mechanic is a familiar coupon rather than a category-original response device.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the in-forest grocery-aisle artefact is a tight ambient-direct idea, but the board offers only three runner quotes in place of any counted response or sales metric, capping the impact case at the lower band.

one_liner: DoorDash delivers every product in every Super Bowl ad.
search_doc:
DoorDash delivers every product in every Super Bowl ad.
Mechanisms: competitor-ad-piggyback, ultra-long-promo-code-cta, real-time-response-mechanic, influencer-led-narration
Idea: To keep people anticipating our spot, we created an unmissable mix of content that connected Super Bowl teasers and ads to our corresponding prize cart items. For those who tuned in, we gave them a simple mission with a not-so-simple promo code: enter and you could win all of it. DoorDash All-The-Ads.com and our LBS character promo code for a chance to win stuff from all the Super Bowl commercials. This turned every interaction into a chance to engage. Every social moment was tracked and reported on. The narrative even further across TikTok and Instagram, we entited a chaotically charming influence to be our 'DoorpDashioners,' who reported on all the ads and corresponding prizes.
Insight: For Super Bowl 58, we wanted to tell the world that DoorDash was more than just a food delivery service. So we developed a media plan that delivered anything you need. So to prove this in the biggest, most audacious way possible, we decided that DoorDash would, if won, fulfill every product placed in every Super Bowl ad on game day during the game to one lucky person. Yes, that means all the cars, the snacks, a horse saddle, $500K for a downpayment on a house, $200K for a dream vacation, plus all the products from the rest of the ads.

The promo-code-as-CTA is a clean, counted entry action.

strategy.rationale: DoorDash is the leading US last-mile delivery platform expanding beyond restaurants into general retail; using the Super Bowl to reposition as 'we deliver everything' is mission-fit with the brand's category extension. The audience choice (100M+ Super Bowl viewers) is appropriate for a mass-reach reframe, though the strategic argument rests on category-extension claim rather than verifiable insight about how Americans currently perceive DoorDash; no consumer-research data backs the perception gap on the board.

The execution exploits the broadcast moment well, though the ultra-long-promo-code mechanic adds entry friction that depresses conversion — a known trade-off the campaign accepts for talkability.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the Super Bowl piggyback is clever and the 8M submissions are a real counted response, but the meaningful DoorDash business outcome (orders, downloads, new actives) is absent and the rest is reach-and-share-of-voice.

one_liner: One promo code delivers every Super Bowl ad's product.
search_doc:
One promo code delivers every Super Bowl ad's product.
Mechanisms: competitor-ad-piggyback, real-time-response-mechanic, ultra-long-promo-code-cta, live-televised-call-to-action
Idea: Every time another brand's Super Bowl ad debuted, DoorDash added their products into our online cart, so the world could keep track in real time. Then, after four straight quarters of brand, influencer, and internet engagement during the game, DoorDash's own Super Bowl ad aired, featuring an incredibly long promo code that you had to enter correctly for a chance to get everything advertised.
Insight: DoorDash was renowned for restaurant delivery, but not for the millions of other products now offered on the app. So during the Super Bowl, when the other brands ran their own ads, DoorDash saw an opportunity to prove that it could now deliver pretty much anything — by delivering all the products, from all the other Super Bowl ads, all to one person.

extracted.insight: DoorDash was renowned for restaurant delivery, but not for the millions of other products now offered on the app. So during the Super Bowl, when the other brands ran their own ads, DoorDash saw an opportunity to prove that it could now deliver pretty much anything — by delivering all the products, from all the other Super Bowl ads, all to one person.
extracted.idea: Every time another brand's Super Bowl ad debuted, DoorDash added their products into our online cart, so the world could keep track in real time. Then, after four straight quarters of brand, influencer, and internet engagement during the game, DoorDash's own Super Bowl ad aired, featuring an incredibly long promo code that you had to enter correctly for a chance to get everything advertised.
execution: Live online cart updated in real time with every product featured in a Super Bowl spot
Custom Super Bowl ad featuring an exceptionally long promo code as the call-to-action
Engagement with influencer and brand content over all four quarters of the game
Multi-brand collaboration to clear legal/IP for every product included
Official and unofficial brand partnerships sealed during the activation
press_coverage: ABC News ("DoorDash Crushed The Super Bowl.")

inferred.context: During the Super Bowl, brands fight for cultural attention with their own commercials, but DoorDash needed to reposition itself in viewers' minds from restaurant delivery to everything delivery.
inferred.one_liner: One promo code delivers every Super Bowl ad's product.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Hijacked the entire Super Bowl ad break by promising to deliver every advertised product, generating 11.9B impressions and 8M submissions for a 76-brand single-code activation.

visual.style_description: Cinematic CGI sunset hero scene piling every Super Bowl-advertised product into a giant DoorDash pickup pyramid, with bold red banner typography across the top; three large red metric blocks divide the page, an OOH/film thumbnail strip sits in the middle, and Insight/Idea/Results columns anchor the bottom.

idea.rationale: Promising to deliver every product advertised in every other Super Bowl ad — for free — is a category-defining response mechanic that hijacks the most expensive ad break in the world and turns it into a single live shopping cart. The promo-code-as-call-to-action turns a passive TV moment into a measurable conversion event, which is precisely what Direct is about.

strategy.rationale: DoorDash is the dominant US on-demand delivery platform that historically meant 'restaurant delivery' but has expanded into everything from groceries to consumer electronics, and this campaign is exact mission-fit: proving the breadth of the catalogue using a cultural moment where the breadth of advertised products is itself the content. The insight — that Super Bowl viewers consume ads with the same intent as the game — is observable rather than data-backed on the board, but the strategic frame (own all ads, not buy a bigger ad) is coherent with the brand's challenger positioning against legacy retail.

execution.rationale: The execution is precision-orchestrated across four quarters of live TV: a real-time cart updated as each spot aired, influencer and brand engagement during the game, then a custom DoorDash spot delivering the ultra-long promo code as the climax. The 76-partner clearance operation behind the activation is itself a craft feat — legally booking IP for every advertised product in the broadcast window is a hard logistical execution that few competitors could pull off.

impact.rationale: 8 million submissions is a counted response action — the right business outcome for a Direct campaign — and 2,468 individual products delivered is a tangible operational metric. The 11.9 billion impressions claim is entrant-reported and unaudited, and the board does not disclose conversion rate (submissions vs. ad viewers) or sales lift from new SKU categories, which would be the harder business proof. Press across NBC, TIME and ABC News is credible breadth of earned conversation.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the promo-code-as-live-CTA converts the Super Bowl's most passive media moment into 8M counted submissions, proving DoorDash can deliver everything by literally doing so on the night.

extracted.tagline: The first earthquake warning system powered by pet health data.
extracted.context: In 2023, 66,900 people died in earthquakes, many of which were felt in Lima, Peru — located on the Pacific Ring of Fire, where 90% of the world's seismic activity occurs. And even with today's technology, people still can't predict an earthquake. But what if animals could? There is scientific evidence that animals change their behavior even hours before an earthquake.
extracted.idea: PetPace, the leader in animal health monitoring, teamed up with London South Bank University to develop Animal Alerts — the first earthquake warning system powered by pet health data. Using integrated media to alert residents in real-time.
execution: Smart collars measure pet temperature, pulse and heart-rate variability
Algorithm detects abnormal stress patterns across multiple dogs simultaneously
Alerts sent to Lima residents via geo-targeted mobile notifications, banners, digital OOH and radio
Pet stores recruit pet owners and dogs into the program
Validated by Dr. Rachel Grant (London South Bank University) on historical dog behaviour records (Peru 2007, Japan 2011, Turkey 2023)

inferred.insight: Animals consistently change their behaviour hours before an earthquake; a continuously-monitored network of pet collars can read that signal at population scale and turn it into a citywide alert.
inferred.one_liner: Dogs become Lima's earthquake early-warning network.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Re-engineers a pet wearables network into a citywide seismic-warning service, alerting 9 million Lima residents across 18 detected events.

visual.style_description: Dramatic black background with a hero side-profile photograph of a black dog wearing the red-and-black PetPace alert collar; the title sits on a glowing seismic-line graphic, with text columns on either side, a small Lima map plus heart-rate iconography for results, and a press-logo strip at the bottom.

idea.rationale: Repurposing a network of pet health collars as a citywide earthquake early-warning system is an original sensor-network reframe — animals as the alert layer humans cannot replicate. The response mechanic (geo-targeted alert to Lima residents) is a clear utility with a counted output.

strategy.rationale: PetPace is a US-Israeli pet-health-monitoring brand whose smart-collar product line positions it around continuous animal vitals, so leveraging its installed sensor base for public good is plausible mission-fit, though earthquake warning sits adjacent to its core veterinary use case rather than at its centre. The context is data-backed — 66,900 earthquake deaths in 2023, Lima on the Pacific Ring of Fire where 90% of seismic activity occurs — and the science is co-signed by Dr Rachel Grant at London South Bank University using historical records from Peru 2007, Japan 2011 and Turkey 2023. Audience is geographically precise: Lima residents.

execution.rationale: Orchestration combines collar telemetry, an anomaly-detection algorithm reading stress patterns across multiple dogs, and a multi-channel alert layer (geo-targeted mobile notifications, banners, DOOH and radio) with pet stores acting as enrolment points. Channel mix is fit for citywide alerting, but the board does not clarify the size of the deployed pet-collar network in Lima — the load-bearing input for whether the warning system actually scales.

impact.rationale: The +9 million residents alerted is the right business outcome for a public-warning utility but is the alert reach rather than a verified accurate-alert rate. The 18 seismic events detected is a small absolute number and the board does not disclose true-positive vs false-alarm performance, the most jury-credible metric for a prediction system. Press across Forbes, CNN, Mashable, El País and Fast Company is a credible earned-conversation signal.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the animal-as-sensor early-warning system is a genuinely original utility with academic co-signing, but the impact case relies on alert-reach numbers rather than predictive-accuracy evidence.

extracted.tagline: A food delivery app turned into a football streaming station.

64 games broadcasted inside the app
metrics: +23% bump in orders compared to the previous month
21M minutes of people who spent an average

inferred.one_liner: A food delivery app becomes a football broadcaster.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned Brazil's biggest food-delivery app into a live broadcaster of the FIFA Women's World Cup; drove +23% order bump, 64 games streamed and 3.5B views, exposing 50M users to women's football.

visual.style_description: Dark sports broadcast aesthetic with iFood's red wordmark and a hero phone mockup showing a Brazil vs Jamaica match streaming inside the food-delivery interface; orange and pink callouts list metrics like +23% orders, +21M minutes and 3.5B views around the hero device.

idea.rationale: Turning a food-delivery app into a live broadcaster of the FIFA Women's World Cup is a clean platform-jailbreak idea that uses iFood's owned attention surface to solve a real distribution gap — no Brazilian free-to-air broadcaster picked up all the games. The mechanism turns the daily 38-minute browse window into a women's-football viewing channel for 50M users.

strategy.rationale: iFood is LATAM's biggest food-delivery app whose strategic remit increasingly extends beyond restaurants into broader lifestyle utility, so positioning the app as a women's-football broadcaster is a credible mission extension though it is not yet exact mission-fit. The context is well-sourced — 93% of Brazilians watched the Men's World Cup vs. only 26% planning to watch the Women's tournament with no open-TV carriage — which is a specific, verifiable gender-coverage gap that the campaign legitimately addresses.

execution.rationale: The orchestration partners iFood with star football streamer Casimiro Miguel for credibility, embeds live streams of 64 games inside the existing app browse flow, and exposes the activation to the entire 50M-user base while they shop for food. Channel choice is fit-for-purpose because the brand's owned audience is larger than most TV broadcasters in Brazil; the integration with Casimiro is a credible amplification layer.

impact.rationale: +23% order bump month-over-month is the right business outcome for a food-delivery app and a genuine response metric, but the comparison period and category isolation are not detailed. +20M users and 3.5B views are entrant-reported reach figures, not audited; 21M minutes and +28% time-in-app are surrogate engagement metrics. The campaign's gender-equality objective (women's football viewership lift) is conspicuously not quantified — neither hours streamed per user nor uplift in women's-football interest is shown — leaving the social-mission outcome weaker than the order-bump outcome.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the app-as-broadcaster mechanic is on-brief and the order bump is a genuine business outcome, but the jury appears to have been harsher than the read on the strength of the cultural intervention given the missing women's-football viewership outcome.

The 'Your ball has arrived' chat-bubble lands the metaphor instantly and is native to the brand's delivery promise.

The strategic soft spot is that the board provides little verifiable context or audience-segment reasoning beyond 'adboards get tuned out' — the insight is plausible but asserted, and the audience scope (Brazilian Série A viewers vs the broader LATAM customer base) is not articulated.

execution.rationale: The activation is native to broadcast: the response mechanic plays inside the natural flow of the match without interrupting it, with the 'Your ball has arrived' message triggered each time a ball enters the box-shaped opening. Roll-out across Brazilian Série A games gives consistent live-broadcast surface area, though the CTA itself is brand-impression rather than a counted action — viewers see the joke but don't take a measurable step.

impact.rationale: The 1.6 billion impressions, 40MM earned views and $6MM earned media value are reach claims (entrant-reported, unaudited) rather than counted responses — pure media value rather than purchases, app installs or app engagement on Mercado Livre itself. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the format-as-message hack is genuinely original and native to live broadcast, but the impact case rests on reach and earned-media value rather than a counted commercial response.

search_doc:
Stand inside a banning library, read the banned book.
Mechanisms: geolocation-as-protest, rights-circumvention, library-as-loophole, legislative-advocacy
Idea: The Banned Book Club is designed for today and for the future. Whenever a new book is banned, it can be added to our dynamic database, ensuring it's available instantly to bring library to anyone in the country.
Insight: Using DPLA America's Index of Book Bans, the Digital Public Library of America made the most powerful weapon against banned books in the U.S. There, geofenced the exact footprint of every library with banned books, so that any person who walks into those libraries gains instant access to the banned book.

extracted.context: Intellectual freedom is under attack in America. More books than ever are being banned from library shelves. Over the past year, thousands of titles have been forced to ban in 33 states.
extracted.insight: Using DPLA America's Index of Book Bans, the Digital Public Library of America made the most powerful weapon against banned books in the U.S. There, geofenced the exact footprint of every library with banned books, so that any person who walks into those libraries gains instant access to the banned book.
extracted.idea: The Banned Book Club is designed for today and for the future. Whenever a new book is banned, it can be added to our dynamic database, ensuring it's available instantly to bring library to anyone in the country.
execution: DPLA America's Index of Book Bans driving the title list
Geofences placed on every library that has banned books
Free instant access to those titles via the DPLA app at the geofenced location
Outdoor surfaced via Obama endorsement and library activations
'Let Freedom Read' rallying call across communications

inferred.one_liner: Stand inside a banning library, read the banned book.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Geofenced 2,314 US libraries that had banned books, granting any visitor instant access to the suppressed titles and unlocking 3,059 individual books across 1.5B local impressions backed by Barack Obama.

visual.style_description: Dark editorial layout with a hand holding up a smartphone whose screen displays a banned-book cover marked with bars; left column carries problem and idea text in a green-on-black palette, the right column stacks five large bright numerical metrics, and a press strip with an Obama endorsement photo runs along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Geofencing every US library that has banned a book, so a visitor inside that library gains instant free access to the banned title through the DPLA app, is a rights-circumvention mechanism that turns the site of suppression into the site of access. The CTA is unmistakable: stand inside the library, unlock the book.

strategy.rationale: The Digital Public Library of America is a US non-profit federating digital library collections, whose stated mission is universal access to knowledge — making banned books readable inside the banning libraries is the most direct possible expression of that mission. The context is data-anchored on the board (thousands of titles banned in 33 states) and supported by DPLA's own America's Index of Book Bans, which strengthens insight veracity; the Obama endorsement adds institutional credibility but is itself a claim the board does not source in detail.

execution.rationale: Geofences on 2,314 US libraries, free instant access via the DPLA app at each location, a dynamic database that auto-adds newly banned titles, plus an Obama-led outdoor amplification — the response architecture is end-to-end and built for ongoing use rather than a stunt window. The 'Let Freedom Read' anchor and library activations give the campaign coordinated entry points though the actual response (download DPLA, walk into library, open the book) carries non-trivial friction.

impact.rationale: 3,059 total books unbanned and 2,314 libraries geofenced are anchored output counts but they describe campaign infrastructure, not user-driven responses; the board does not disclose how many people actually used the app to read a banned title — the behaviour-change outcome that would prove the mechanic worked. 1.5B local impressions is an entrant-reported reach figure, and 'libraries-books unbanned' / 'books on racial issues unbanned' read as scope re-statements rather than independent counted responses.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the geofenced-library loophole is a sharp, mission-perfect mechanism, but the response evidence is infrastructure scale (libraries covered, books listed) rather than counted reader access.

one_liner: Beer points become a retirement plan for Colombian shopkeepers.
search_doc:
Beer points become a retirement plan for Colombian shopkeepers.
Mechanisms: loyalty-points-repurposed, branded-financial-utility, informal-economy-pension, b2b-platform-jailbreak
Idea: Seeking a solution, Poker decided to make a small change on BEES, AB InBev's B2B platform already familiar to shopkeepers, where they buy beer for their businesses and earn points redeemable for prizes. However, thousands of these points were going unused and expiring.

extracted.insight: According to the 2023 Global Retirement Index, Colombia's pension system is one of the world's worst. Approximately 82% of informal workers like shopkeepers will never retire, as reported by Colombia's National Administrative Department of Statistics. Poker, known as the beer brand of friendship, acknowledged this dire situation and decided to support the future of its key partners: the shopkeepers.
extracted.idea: Seeking a solution, Poker decided to make a small change on BEES, AB InBev's B2B platform already familiar to shopkeepers, where they buy beer for their businesses and earn points redeemable for prizes. However, thousands of these points were going unused and expiring.
execution: Modified BEES B2B app to add a special retirement savings account for shopkeepers
Unused BEES loyalty points automatically transferred to those retirement accounts
Partnership with the Colombian Government and the Colombian Pension Fund (Colpensiones) to create the Beer Retirement Account
Initiative empowering shopkeepers to convert their unused BEES points into real money for retirement
metrics: +12.5% Poker orders are increasing among shopkeepers

inferred.one_liner: Beer points become a retirement plan for Colombian shopkeepers.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Converted dormant beer-loyalty points into the first retirement plan for 10,000+ Colombian informal-economy shopkeepers, lifting BEES adoption 21% and unlocking $271.4M in redistributed points.

visual.style_description: Hero portrait of a smiling shopkeeper inside a tienda, holding a phone showing the Beer Retirement Account screen with the campaign lockup overlaid; warm orange-yellow palette throughout, sponsorship lockup with AB InBev and Poker centered, headline at top in white and yellow, six bold metric blocks across the bottom above a strip of corner-shop photos.

idea.rationale: Converting dormant beer-loyalty points into actual retirement savings for informal-economy shopkeepers is a category-original repurposing of a B2B platform. The response mechanism is mundane and inevitable — every order earns BEES points that now auto-route to a pension, so participation requires no behavior change beyond opting in.

strategy.rationale: Poker is AB InBev's flagship Colombian beer brand whose 'cerveza de la amistad' (beer of friendship) positioning anchors it to the shopkeeper community that distributes it, so building a pension for those same shopkeepers is exact mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The insight is data-backed on the board — Colombia's pension system ranks among the world's worst per the 2023 Global Retirement Index, and ~82% of informal workers (including shopkeepers) will never retire per DANE — a credible, sourced diagnosis. Audience is precisely identified: the 550,000 corner shops representing 40% of national mass-consumer market share.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs through existing infrastructure: a feature added to BEES, the B2B app shopkeepers already use, with unused loyalty points auto-routed into a partnered Colpensiones pension account. The channel choice is right because the mechanic lives where the audience already transacts daily, and the government + Colpensiones partnership provides regulatory legitimacy rather than relying on paid amplification.

impact.rationale: Business outcomes are concrete with absolute bases: +10,000 shopkeepers actively saving, +$271.4M in BEES points redistributed, $480,000 in net pension savings, +20% government top-up contribution. The +12.5% Poker order lift and +21% BEES adoption are credible direct-response numbers because they tie back to the loyalty mechanic itself, though the board carries no press coverage to corroborate the scale claim. The 10,000-shopkeeper number is small against the 550,000-shop universe, which limits the systemic-change story.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the points-as-pension mechanic is genuinely original and mission-fit for Poker's shopkeeper community, but the 10,000-shopkeeper enrollment is modest against the 550,000-shop universe the strategy frames.

idea.rationale: Loaning real Renaults free of charge for the duration of a new hire's three-month trial period is a barrier-removal response mechanism that converts the brand's dealership network into a public mobility service. The 'no car, no job; no job, no car; no loan, no car' framing names the exact response barrier the product then dismantles.

strategy.rationale: Renault Group is France's incumbent national carmaker, whose post-2020 strategy reframes mobility as a public service; lending cars to job-seekers in mobility deserts is direct mission-fit with that positioning, not borrowed purpose. The insight is data-anchored on the board with specific verifiable figures (40% of French people live without public transport; 54% of job-seekers have refused a position for lack of mobility), and the French three-month trial-period credit lock-out is a real, named regulatory friction the offer is engineered around.

execution.rationale: The response is built into channels job-seekers actually use: a fleet of 6,000 vehicles distributed through 50 dedicated Renault dealerships in mobility deserts and listed on FranceTravail.fr, the French state employment service. The public-private partnership with FranceTravail makes the CTA discoverable inside the job-search flow itself, with broad national press relay amplifying eligibility awareness.

impact.rationale: 6,000 cars made available and 50 dealerships converted are absolute, anchored business outputs — the right response numbers for a barrier-removal programme. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because Renault re-engineers its own dealer network into a state-backed employment service, with the barrier (trial-period credit lock-out) named precisely and the response infrastructure (6,000 cars, 50 dealerships, FranceTravail integration) counted in absolute terms.

Mechanisms: reverse-call-to-action, category-rule-inversion, product-as-investment, ritualised-restraint
Idea: Hertog Jan launched a campaign telling people to NOT drink their beer. Through years of research, the brewery realised their beer has a more mature, complex flavour when properly stored and aged. So we DON'T DRINK HERTOG JAN to reveal that nuanced, deeply aromatic character through time. We need a different type of consumer, one that buys Hertog Jan to save for special occasions, family moments, or just for the love of beer.

visual.style_description: Cellar-lit hero shot of a dusty aged Hertog Jan bottle next to a phone showing the storage tracker, with the campaign's blackletter 'don't drink Hertog Jan' wordmark dominating the centre in gold; dark wood backdrop, a narrative left column of body text, four gold metric callouts on the right, and a bottom strip of aged-bottle vignettes labelled by year.

idea.rationale: Telling consumers not to drink your beer is a clean inversion of the entire beer-category convention and re-positions the SKU as a cellar-able investment rather than a consumption item. The 'don't drink, age' instruction is itself the call-to-action — the response is to buy and store, which is unusual and ownable for a mainstream lager challenging Heineken.

strategy.rationale: Hertog Jan is the Arcen-based Dutch heritage brewery (now part of AB InBev) positioned as the connoisseur counter-point to Heineken's mass-market dominance, so leaning into restraint, ageing and depth of flavour is exact mission-fit for the brand's craft-beer-as-everyday-luxury frame. The context — Heineken's category dominance and Hertog Jan's resource asymmetry — is plausible and known in the Dutch market; the ageing-improves-flavour claim is presented as 'years of research' but is not externally sourced on the board.

execution.rationale: The activation routes the reverse-CTA through a paired tracker (smartphone-based aging app + in-store displays of aged vs fresh bottles), with brewer-led storage events that give the campaign a narrative arc over time. Distributing across 23 supermarkets with dedicated resting fixtures is the right retail-led channel for a category where shelf presence drives the actual purchase decision; the orchestration of physical retail, app and ambassador events is coherent if not breakthrough.

impact.rationale: '#1 in share' and '#1 in preference' are the right business outcomes for a beer brand, but the board concedes in the same line that Heineken still outsells Hertog Jan ('the second-best selling beer'), which contradicts the headline framing and softens the claim. '31,983 m² resting in 23 stores' and '12,004 years of resting beer' are evocative but cumulative shelf-space metrics, not counted responses; absolute sales lift or new-buyer numbers are not disclosed.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the reverse-CTA is a credibly category-breaking mechanism that fits the brand's connoisseur position, but the headline 'most sold' framing internally contradicts the second-place admission on the same board.

Mechanisms: ambient-media-hijack, fan-as-cocreator, earned-media-leverage, promo-as-treasure-hunt
Idea: Burger King launched a completely invisible campaign — just the 'ghosts' — encouraging people to take photos of places as if there were Burger King ads there.
Insight: Halloween belongs to ghosts; budget-free brands can hijack the imagination by leaving blank space for fans to fill in.

extracted.tagline: Share invisible ads, win real food.
extracted.context: On Halloween, brands launch big, splashy expensive activations. But, in 2023 we had to get people's attention with a budget of $0.
extracted.idea: Burger King launched a completely invisible campaign — just the 'ghosts' — encouraging people to take photos of places as if there were Burger King ads there.
execution: Invisible Halloween ads released at locations across cities
People photograph empty spots as if Burger King ads were there
Each ghost ad reveals a Whopper, Long Chickens or Big Kings reward via secret promo code
Twitter / X conversations and social posts featured the 'ghost' shots
Spotted via the BK app to redeem
press_coverage: Yahoo: "Can't decide if it's dumb or brilliant"
Campaign: "You could be rewarded with a tangible treat"

inferred.insight: Halloween belongs to ghosts; budget-free brands can hijack the imagination by leaving blank space for fans to fill in.
inferred.one_liner: Invisible Halloween ads only the most loyal fans can see.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Released invisible Halloween ads with $0 media spend; fans imagined Burger King creatives at empty locations and unlocked promo codes, driving +6% sales, sold-out codes and $4.5M saved in media on 99% positive sentiment.

visual.style_description: Dark blue ghostly aesthetic with the title 'The Ghost Campaign' rendered in glowing white type; hero phone mockup shows a fan-shot of a blank wall with a faint Whopper outline; a row of social posts framed as 'ghost' sightings sits above with metric blocks across the top.

idea.rationale: Releasing invisible Halloween 'ghost' ads — empty locations fans are invited to photograph as if a Burger King creative were there — is a fan-as-cocreator mechanism that turns blank space into a reward-redemption CTA. The mechanic doubles as participation and earned media on a $0 budget.

strategy.rationale: Burger King is the global QSR challenger brand whose long-running positioning relies on culturally responsive, low-budget creativity (Whopper Detour, Moldy Whopper, etc.), so a zero-budget invisible Halloween activation is exactly on-brief for the brand's signature stance. The strategic underpinning — that BK can hijack Halloween imagination without media spend — is plausible and brand-true; the board does not articulate a specific audience-need beyond 'attention with $0', leaving the insight more brand-stance than research-backed.

execution.rationale: The response loop runs through fan-photographed empty locations, X/Twitter posts, BK-app secret promo codes and Whopper / Big King / Long Chicken redemptions — a tight participation-to-redemption chain. Channels are owned (BK app, social) plus earned, suiting the $0 brief, but the visibility relies entirely on whether fans organically participate, which the board treats as a result rather than an executional risk.

impact.rationale: 'Sold out promo codes' is a clean counted response in spirit but the absolute number of codes is not disclosed — 'sold out of what cohort?' remains open. +6% sales and $4.5M media saved are anchored figures, the right CPG business outcomes; 99% positive brand sentiment is an unanchored percentage of an undisclosed measurement base, and the press strip (Yahoo, Taxi, Campaign) is small relative to a global BK earned-media programme.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the invisible-ad mechanic is on-brief for BK's zero-budget challenger stance and produced a +6% sales bump, but the response volume ('sold out' without a base) is undisclosed and impact rests on a small earned-press footprint.

one_liner: The donor's voice overrides the family's grief.
search_doc:
The donor's voice overrides the family's grief.
Mechanisms: voice-as-legal-consent, behaviour-change-call-to-action, government-adopted-service, data-as-content
Idea: Hear My Last Wish is a simple yet powerful digital service designed in collaboration between Taiwan Organ Sharing Registry and Patient Autonomy Promotion Center and the Taiwan Ministry of Health and Welfare. It allows potential donors to record their donation wishes in audio format, played at crucial moments during hospital consent-seeking, ensuring the smooth execution of their intentions.
Insight: Even signed consent forms require final family approval for organ transplantation. While this policy aims to verify authenticity and prevent disputes, many individuals remain unaware of the deceased's donation wishes due to societal avoidance of the topic. Fueled by doubt and grief, up to 99% of living relatives may refuse consent during hospital consent-seeking, leading to organ donation procedure failures.

extracted.tagline: The voiceprint database revolutionizing organ donation consent. Officially included in the government's organ donation process by Taiwan Ministry of Health and Welfare.
extracted.context: Taiwan, the first in Asia to enact organ transplant laws, sees consistently high and growing annual consent rates. Despite widespread societal acceptance, Taiwan's annual successful donation cases (10 per million people) still lag far behind other countries.
extracted.insight: Even signed consent forms require final family approval for organ transplantation. While this policy aims to verify authenticity and prevent disputes, many individuals remain unaware of the deceased's donation wishes due to societal avoidance of the topic. Fueled by doubt and grief, up to 99% of living relatives may refuse consent during hospital consent-seeking, leading to organ donation procedure failures.
extracted.idea: Hear My Last Wish is a simple yet powerful digital service designed in collaboration between Taiwan Organ Sharing Registry and Patient Autonomy Promotion Center and the Taiwan Ministry of Health and Welfare. It allows potential donors to record their donation wishes in audio format, played at crucial moments during hospital consent-seeking, ensuring the smooth execution of their intentions.
execution: Voiceprint audio recording captured at point of donor registration
Audio file stored in the national organ donation database
Played to grieving family members during hospital consent-seeking
Officially adopted into the government's organ donation process
Endorsed by the Taiwan Ministry of Health and Welfare
metrics: +34.6% organ donation successful cases, reached a decade-high

inferred.one_liner: The donor's voice overrides the family's grief.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Adopted by Taiwan's Ministry of Health into the national organ donation process, lifting consent signing by 127%, driving a decade-high in successful donation cases and adding 42 hospitals to the network.

visual.style_description: Cinematic split-portrait of two grieving faces facing each other across a centered smartphone showing a recording waveform; muted warm tones throughout, with metrics row and three labelled text blocks (Background, Problem, Idea) running beneath the hero.

idea.rationale: Capturing donors' voice-recorded last wishes to play at the hospital consent moment is a precise emotional-evidence mechanic — the donor's own voice overrides the family's grief at the exact decision point where consent is most likely to collapse. The response mechanic ties registration to a small, concrete action: record your audio.

strategy.rationale: The Taiwan Organ Sharing Registry is the national body managing organ donor consent in Taiwan, with the explicit mission of converting signed donor intent into successful transplants, so the work is exact mission-fit. The insight is data-backed and culturally precise — Taiwan was first in Asia to legislate organ transplantation and has growing consent rates, but achieves only ~10 successful donations per million people because up to 99% of relatives refuse consent at the hospital, driven by societal avoidance of the topic. Audience targeting is sharply defined: existing registry signatories and grieving families at the consent moment.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs through partnership with the Patient Autonomy Promotion Center and Taiwan Ministry of Health and Welfare to formally adopt the audio-consent layer into the national donation process across 42 transplant hospitals. The channel choice — the hospital consent interview itself — is the only place the mechanic could matter, and integration with the government database gives it institutional permanence rather than campaign-window life.

impact.rationale: The 3,562+ recorded wishes is a concrete absolute number tied to the desired behaviour. The +34.6% lift in successful donation cases (decade-high) is a credible business outcome but lacks the baseline cohort size, and the +127% consent-signing rate is unbased — '127% of what baseline?' is open. The $1.2M media value is an entrant-reported PR equivalency rather than a verified result, and the board carries no third-party press logos to corroborate the scale.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the voice-as-consent layer is structurally elegant and has been formally adopted into Taiwan's national donation process, but the headline lift numbers rest on unbased percentages without clear baselines.

We even doubled the bottles to create an X-shaped bottle, taking aim at hype culture's most ubiquitous symbol for collaborations: the 'X'.

idea.rationale: A 'self-collab' — Hidden Valley Ranch x Hidden Valley Ranch in an X-shaped twin-bottle pack — is a sharp parody of hype-drop culture that uses the genre's own visual grammar to short-circuit it. The X-bottle is a single-frame readable artefact and the response is the actual purchase, which is the cleanest possible Direct mechanic.

strategy.rationale: Hidden Valley Ranch is the US category-leading ranch-dressing brand whose long-running platform plays seriously with the cultural fixation around the dressing, so leaning into hype-drop parody fits the brand's established self-aware tone. The strategic premise — that a brand can short-circuit drop culture by collaborating with itself — is observational rather than data-backed, and the board does not specify who the 'next generation of Hidden Valley Ranch consumers' is in audience terms.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is product-led: a special-edition Double Ranch SKU in an X-shaped twin-bottle pack, sold through Hidden Valley Ranch's own channels, supported by influencer / press seeding and late-night TV pickup. Channel choice (limited-edition direct-to-consumer drop + earned TV) is fit-for-purpose for a parody of hype-drop mechanics, and the X-bottle packaging is itself the executional payoff.

impact.rationale: 8.6% increase in bottle sales is the right business outcome for an FMCG and is a credibly anchored claim, though the comparison window and SKU isolation are not detailed. 'Sold out in less than 30 minutes' is a real counted response on the limited-edition drop, but the actual unit volume is not disclosed — 'sold out of how many?' remains open. 1.9B impressions is an entrant-reported reach claim; the late-night and morning-show pickups (Jimmy Fallon, Mario Lopez) are credible earned-conversation signals.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the self-collab parody is a tight one-frame idea backed by a real sales lift, but the jury appears slightly harsher than the read given the unanchored 'sold out in 30 minutes' claim and unaudited 1.9B impressions.

campaign: If you're into it, it's in the V&A
one_liner: Every subculture already has objects in the V&A.
search_doc:
Every subculture already has objects in the V&A.
Mechanisms: hyper-niche-targeting, object-as-direct-mail, subculture-treasure-hunt, collection-as-content
Idea: Ludicrously targeted direct activations sent to fans of specific subcultures, each pointing them to the V&A object that secretly belongs to their world. Hidden, seeded and leaked objects with codes immediately directed the finder to the V&A objects relevant to their passions.
Insight: The campaign harnesses the breadth of the V&A collection: people into trainers, gaming, fitness, queer culture, K-pop or any niche have dozens of authentic objects in the museum's archive sitting just for them.

full_record.file_path: 2024/DIRECT/SILVER/If You_re Into It It_s In The - V&A (adam&eveDDB, London)_.jpg

extracted.campaign: If you're into it, it's in the V&A
extracted.tagline: The most ludicrously targeted campaign in history.
extracted.context: Only 18.5% of people in the UK have visited a museum in the last 12 months, because they don't think they have anything they'd be interested in. But the V&A museum has 2.8 million objects spanning thousands of years of human creativity, so there's something for everyone in its collection.
extracted.insight: The campaign harnesses the breadth of the V&A collection: people into trainers, gaming, fitness, queer culture, K-pop or any niche have dozens of authentic objects in the museum's archive sitting just for them.
extracted.idea: Ludicrously targeted direct activations sent to fans of specific subcultures, each pointing them to the V&A object that secretly belongs to their world. Hidden, seeded and leaked objects with codes immediately directed the finder to the V&A objects relevant to their passions.
execution: Hidden, seeded, leaked, locked sweaters, hacked livestreams, smuggled puzzles, branded posters
QR-coded objects placed inside the audiences' favourite places (eBay/Discogs to a left-foot puzzle store)
Live treasure-hunt activations inside London Underground stations
OOH placed in unexpected micro-locations (a tram station's hardware shop, etc.)
Each stunt led to the corresponding V&A collection object
By targeting one interest at a time, from the mainstream to the super-niche, the V&A reached millions.

inferred.one_liner: Every subculture already has objects in the V&A.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Hundreds of niche, hyper-targeted stunts directed each subculture audience to the V&A object they didn't know they shared with the museum, lifting collection searches by 12,000% and V&A searches by 23%.

visual.style_description: Dense grid of small product/object photographs (sneakers, books, posters, jars, plant pots, t-shirts) on a black background, with the bright yellow headline 'If you're into it, it's in the V&A' anchoring the top-left corner; metrics row sits along the bottom with press logos beneath.

idea.rationale: 'If you're into it, it's in the V&A' is a sharp creative platform that converts the museum's 2.8M-object archive into a personalised proof-point for every niche subculture, then routes each subculture to its specific object via a hyper-targeted stunt. The mechanism scales: hundreds of bespoke executions all collapse into the same one-line idea, which is the hallmark of a strong direct platform.

strategy.rationale: The V&A is London's national museum of art, design and performance, whose stated remit is 'to champion creativity and inspire designers everywhere', so reframing the collection as a living catalogue of every subculture's objects is exact mission-fit. The context — only 18.5% of UK adults visited a museum in the last 12 months — is a specific, sourceable cultural data point grounded in arts-engagement surveys, and the insight (the V&A already contains every niche but visitors don't know it) is observable and verifiable from the collection itself.

execution.rationale: The orchestration runs hundreds of micro-executions tailored to each subculture (sneaker drops, gaming, K-pop, queer culture, fitness), each placed in the audience's native venue (eBay, Discogs, hardware stores, tram stations, Underground treasure hunts) and routed to the relevant V&A collection page via QR. Channel choice is exemplary for a niche-by-niche direct play, and the volume of executions is itself the proof of platform extensibility, though the operational cost of running 'hundreds' of unique placements is not detailed.

impact.rationale: +12,000% rise in collection searches is the right business outcome for a museum trying to convert curiosity into discovery, but the base is conspicuously absent — 12,000% of how many searches per day? — so the load-bearing number is unanchored. +23% increase in searches for V&A is more credible directionally, and 37M campaign impressions is an entrant-reported reach figure. The board does not disclose visit lift, ticket sales or new membership conversions, which would be the harder museum-business outcome.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the 'every subculture is in the V&A' platform scales across hundreds of executions and lifts collection searches sharply, but the headline +12,000% is unanchored and museum-visit business outcome is not disclosed.

one_liner: Hack LinkedIn so Down syndrome candidates rank first.
search_doc:
Hack LinkedIn so Down syndrome candidates rank first.
Mechanisms: algorithm-hijack, platform-jailbreak, purpose-driven-product, disability-inclusion-activation
Idea: Inployable, the world's first employment network for people with Down syndrome on LinkedIn, hacked the algorithm by 'hiring' people with Down syndrome in a company page, giving them instant job experience and ensuring they ranked higher in job searches. We then partnered with LinkedIn to add the skills of the community to the platform so we could change employers perceptions and show they're capable of filling their open jobs.
Insight: LinkedIn ranks candidates by experience — but people with Down syndrome are blocked from gaining experience because of hiring bias. Break the bias by hacking the algorithm.

extracted.tagline: The first employment network on LinkedIn for people with Down Syndrome.
extracted.context: North America is facing a historic labor shortage with over 9 million job vacancies. Yet over 50% of people with Down syndrome can't find paid work. They are all but invisible on hiring platforms like LinkedIn as algorithms prioritise people with job experience in latent searches, which most people with Down syndrome lack, in large part due to hiring bias that they have little to no valuable skills.

We then partnered with LinkedIn to add the skills of the community to the platform so we could change employers perceptions and show they're capable of filling their open jobs.

Targeted major companies with personalised mailers using staff names
OOH highlighting candidate capabilities
Amplifying open jobs on Inployable
Resources for employers
Hey Home Depot personalised CTA mailers
press_coverage: NBC: "A solution to the labour shortage"

inferred.insight: LinkedIn ranks candidates by experience — but people with Down syndrome are blocked from gaining experience because of hiring bias. Break the bias by hacking the algorithm.
inferred.one_liner: Hack LinkedIn so Down syndrome candidates rank first.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Created the first employment network for people with Down syndrome on LinkedIn; 17% of candidates already hired, 700+ companies onboarded across 24 countries, and 91% of followers are HR decision-makers.

idea.rationale: Hacking the LinkedIn algorithm by 'hiring' people with Down syndrome onto a company page — giving them the instant job-experience credential the platform ranks on — is an original algorithm-hijack. The response mechanic is layered: candidates surface in recruiter searches, employers receive personalised mailers, new skill tags rewrite how the community is described.

strategy.rationale: The Canadian Down Syndrome Society is the national NGO advocating for people with Down syndrome, so building an employment infrastructure on LinkedIn is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The context is data-backed on the board — over 9 million job vacancies in North America against 50%+ unemployment for people with Down syndrome — and the insight (LinkedIn's experience-weighted algorithm structurally excludes people without prior employment, perpetuating hiring bias) is a precise diagnosis of why traditional advocacy doesn't move the needle. Audience targeting is sharply defined: HR decision-makers reached on the platform where they already screen candidates.

execution.rationale: Orchestration is multi-layered: a CDSS company page hires the candidates onto LinkedIn directly, a partnership with LinkedIn adds new skill tags (punctual, eager, low absenteeism, mission-oriented, customer service), OOH and personalised mailers (e.g. 'Hey Home Depot') target specific employers, and resources for HR teams support post-hire integration. Channel choice is precise because the mechanic lives where the actual hiring decisions are made.

impact.rationale: The 700+ companies using Inployable, 24 countries and 91% HR-lead follower composition are concrete absolute numbers tied to platform adoption. The '17% of candidates already hired' is a credible response outcome for an employment platform, though the absolute cohort behind it ('17% of how many candidates?') is not disclosed on the board. Press across Yahoo, NBC, CBC and iHeart are entrant-reported coverage claims that signal earned conversation without substantiating the hiring numbers themselves.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the algorithm-hijack is a sharp structural intervention with credible LinkedIn co-signing and HR-lead audience capture, but the 17% hired figure floats without an absolute candidate base.

one_liner: Reviews you can only write after years of use.

Reviews you can only write after years of use.

Idea: Darty changed its review policy and created the first reviews based on long-term customer experience: The Long-Lasting Reviews (Les Avis Longue-Durée).

inferred.one_liner: Reviews you can only write after years of use.

idea.rationale: Reinventing the product review so it can only be written after years of ownership is a structural rewrite of an entire feedback system, not a single piece of content. The response mechanic — write a review you can only earn through long-term use — turns the review form itself into a durability proof point.

strategy.rationale: Darty is the iconic French home-appliance retailer whose 50-year 'commitment to durability' positioning (the famous Contrat de Confiance) anchors its market identity, so a review system that privileges longevity is exact mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The insight is data-backed on the board — 87% of people rely on reviews and 77% of reviews are posted immediately after unboxing — a credible diagnosis of why the existing review ecosystem fails durability-seeking shoppers. Audience targeting is precise: existing Darty customers, addressable through their purchase history.

execution.rationale: Orchestration closes the response loop end-to-end: solicit long-term reviews from existing customers, surface long-lasting grades on product pages, mirror those scores in-store, and feed the data back to manufacturers. The owned-media channels (Darty.com product pages, in-store POS) are the right place to convert the mechanic into purchase intent at point of sale, and manufacturer partnerships extend the system beyond a campaign.

impact.rationale: Business outcomes are well-anchored: 3,845,670 customers engaged and 18,228 reviewable products are absolute numbers, not unbased percentages. The +23 brand-trust and +21 purchase-intent points are tracker-style metrics rather than sales numbers, so the ultimate business outcome (revenue lift) is not shown, but trust and purchase intent are the right proxies for a durability-positioning campaign and the manufacturer endorsement quote signals category-level traction.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the review system itself becomes the durability proof — a structural retail-policy rewrite anchored to Darty's 50-year mission and demonstrated at multi-million-customer scale.

idea.rationale: Posters that physically run away the moment a passerby tries to photograph them — using the show's master-thief signature as the interactive mechanic — is a clean character-behaviour-as-medium idea. Each poster reveals the next stop in the city-wide chase, turning a passive OOH placement into a multi-step response interaction native to the show's premise.

strategy.rationale: Netflix is the global streaming platform whose every season-launch is a discrete audience-acquisition campaign, and 'Lupin' specifically is a French-Paris-anchored heist series where Paris is itself a character, so reframing Parisian outdoor as the chase setting is exact mission-fit. The cultural insight (Lupin defined by escape) is verifiable from the show, but the board offers no audience-size data or viewership baseline to anchor the strategic case for an OOH-led activation.

execution.rationale: The orchestration deploys digital OOH across Paris with smartphone-camera-detection triggering a Lupin escape animation, each panel cuing the next location in a chase narrative, then cross-channel rollout to OOH and social. Channel choice (Paris DOOH for a Paris show) is fit-for-purpose and the tech mechanic is genuinely interactive; the campaign appears to live as a single-city activation rather than a phased global rollout, which is acceptable but limits scale.

impact.rationale: 7.3M global reach and 1.5M impressions are entrant-reported reach figures and modest by Netflix-launch standards; the board carries no business outcome at all — no viewership lift, no completion-rate uplift, no subscription gain, no sign-up data for the new Lupin season, which is the actual business outcome a streamer cares about. There is no press coverage block, leaving the impact case to two reach numbers and no documented audience response.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the chase-the-poster mechanic is character-perfect and technically distinctive, but the impact case rests on two unaudited reach numbers with no viewership-lift outcome for the actual Lupin season launch.

The insight is data-anchored on the board with specific dog-search behaviour data (people search for impossibly low-maintenance dogs), and the categorical mismatch between search demand and reality is a verifiable, observable pattern in pet-search data.

execution.rationale: Search-intent hijacks intercept Google queries for low-maintenance dog breeds and serve the Meowzer page; a Meowzer.dog matching algorithm pairs visitors with a real shelter cat, supported by influencer rollout across cat and dog accounts. why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the search-intent-hijack is sharp, on-brief and culturally clever, but the response case rests on a 27% adoption lift with no absolute base and a consideration multiple that is unanchored.

idea.rationale: Reframing gender bias as a measurable financial loss — by building a live stock index of women-led companies that investors can buy into — is a financial-instrument-as-advocacy mechanism with an unambiguous response (invest). It turns a moral failure into a tradable signal, which is a category-distinctive way for a broker to mobilise its audience.

strategy.rationale: DEGIRO is Europe's largest retail investment platform; partnering with UN Women to launch an index that monetises gender-bias correction is on-brief for a broker whose business is helping retail investors act on data, and the work is explicitly tied to UN SDG 5.5. The context is data-anchored on the board with concrete, citable figures (7% female CEOs of public companies; 2-3% share-price drop on female-CEO appointment), and the named research base gives the strategic claim verifiable footing — soft spot is that those data points are attributed to 'research' without naming the source studies.

execution.rationale: The response asset is a working financial product (Pink Chip U.S. Index), built on a proprietary dataset assembled via AI name-recognition on corporate filings, freely available on PinkChip.org and tradable on the DEGIRO platform — the CTA is direct (compare and invest). The orchestration ties data-product launch, owned-platform integration, comparison vs S&P 500 / male-led benchmark and earned PR (Forbes, Bloomberg) into a single conversion loop targeted at retail investors across 14 countries.

impact.rationale: 14.5k new investors in Pink Chip companies in the EU within three weeks and 67k Pink Chip index uses are absolute counted responses — the right business outcome for a broker. The +80% outperformance over four years and +182.53% vs +101.92% / +88.43% S&P comparison are anchored with concrete numbers but rely on backward-applied index methodology rather than live AUM; reach claims (85M people across 14 countries, 58M impressions) are entrant-reported, and the absence of disclosed AUM or DEGIRO sign-up uplift leaves the broker's direct business outcome partially open.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the index turns a UN gender-equality goal into a tradable product with documented investor sign-ups, but the brokerage's own conversion outcome (AUM, new DEGIRO accounts) is not isolated, keeping the response case one rung below Gold.

The insight that pubs already function as community archives is observable and grounded in the cited closure data.

execution.rationale: The orchestration converts each participating pub into a working virtual museum: a scannable Pub Museum mark, a mobile app of pub-by-pub exhibits, a Heineken-branded audio tour, and a commerce flow for beers and souvenirs all routed through the same interaction. The choice of channels (physical pub signage + utility app + tourism guide) is fit-for-purpose for both the patron audience and the owner-side response (filing for accreditation), and the broad business-press loop across Bloomberg, FT and Irish Examiner amplifies it credibly.

The conversion logic is tight — the very people the brand wants to drive to Drive Thru are the activation's eligible response audience.

strategy.rationale: Burger King is the QSR challenger globally and in Brazil consistently uses provocative humour and cultural appropriation as its strategic positioning, so a body-as-Drive-Thru gag is exact tonal and mission-fit with the brand's playbook. The stated context — that the Drive Thru channel is underused despite broad store coverage — is plausible but the 'almost as many drive-thrus as bald people in Brazil' comparison is rhetorical rather than data-anchored, leaving the strategic premise loosely sourced.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is simple and tight: OOH and social setting up the visual gag, an in-store free-Whopper offer that gates eligibility on scalp match, capture of bald customer arrivals as user-generated content, and a five-day campaign window that creates urgency. Channel choice maps directly to the response objective (drive-to-store), and the participation mechanic is self-amplifying because the customers themselves become the next round of media.

impact.rationale: +500,000 record participants is a counted response, +32% drive-thru sales and +22% general sales lift are the right business outcomes for a QSR direct activation, and both have absolute time-bounds (5 days). +193% more people at Drive Thru is similarly anchored to a usable baseline. +22,000 user-generated videos is a credible engagement metric, and the AdAge / Globo / Contagious press is genre-appropriate breadth.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the body-as-Drive-Thru gag converts directly into counted footfall and a measurable sales lift in five days, even though the strategic premise leans on a rhetorical comparison rather than a sourced number.

idea.rationale: Declaring a National Delay Day and stopping the 600-year-old Prague Astronomical Clock for 13 minutes is a national-ritual creation that dramatises the campaign's behavioural data point: speeding only saves 13 minutes. The response mechanic asks the country to adopt the delay collectively, with cultural institutions providing the permission structure.

strategy.rationale: The Czech Insurance Association is the national industry body representing Czech insurers — a clear business stake in reducing road fatalities, so a national speeding-reduction campaign is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The context and insight are both data-backed on the board: speeding causes 39% of fatal accidents, 43% of Czechs admit they speed because they are in a rush, and the association's own traffic data analysis revealed the average time saved by speeding on the busiest highway is only 13 minutes — a clean evidence chain from problem to mechanic. Audience is national: Czech drivers.

execution.rationale: Orchestration scales the symbolic act: the Astronomical Clock delay (the load-bearing visual), Football Cup semi-finals pushed back, university classes and theatre performances delayed, plus 50+ influencer and 50+ company partners organically joining. The channel mix turns mass-cultural ritual into the medium itself, which is fit for national-norm change in a small country, and the institutional co-signing (the 600-year-old clock breaking precedent for the first time) gives the message authority press releases cannot.

impact.rationale: The headline 9.2% immediate drop in speeding accidents is the right business outcome for a road-safety campaign, though the time window and absolute base are not specified on the board. The 10 million earned media is entrant-reported and unaudited, and the 70% population reach is a surrogate rather than a counted Direct response. The cultural scope is precisely Czech — a national astronomical-clock ritual that does not translate beyond the country — which is honest framing rather than a weakness.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the 13-minute national delay is a culturally precise norm-setting ritual with a clean data-to-mechanic chain, even if the 9.2% accident drop lacks a clearly stated baseline window and the broader reach figures are entrant-reported.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because the recruitment mechanic IS the product demo — the audition turns Football Manager from a simulation into a verifiable real-world coaching pipeline, with Bromley FC's record season as proof.

execution.rationale: The response architecture is straightforward: a Greenprint poster as the central asset, a companion conversion kit, online how-to videos and an FAQ, supported by NZ-market roll-out — a coherent DIY pipeline pointing at one downloadable CTA. Channels suit a small but engaged audience (Kombi enthusiasts in New Zealand), though the campaign is narrow in surface area and lacks the multi-channel amplification of higher-tier Direct work.

impact.rationale: 2,000+ Greenprint downloads is an absolute, anchored counted response and the right direct-action proof, but the cohort is small and there is no disclosure of how many downloads converted into actual completed conversions — the meaningful business/behaviour outcome. '1.7 tonnes CO2 saved per conversion per year' is a per-unit modelled estimate, not a measured aggregate result, and broader business outcomes (Kombi-owner brand-shift, VW EV consideration lift) are not shown.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the open-source EV blueprint is a clean response asset on credible mission-fit, but the response volume (2,000 downloads) is modest and unverified at the actual-conversion step, capping the impact case.

The context is data-backed on the board — Americans spend $12 billion on candy each Halloween, turning the country into a horror-set backdrop — and the rationale for using Uber Eats as a candy-delivery channel is plausible. Audience targeting is sharp: US Halloween candy shoppers and horror-movie fandom, reached through the studio-licensed quote layer.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs studio partnerships across Warner Brothers, Paramount, Lionsgate and MGM to license quotes from It, Scream, Nightmare on Elm Street and Saw, surfaced through OOH and digital placements, then redeemed inside the Uber Eats app's new Halloween mode. The channel mix is fit for an in-app promo activation, and the studio licensing gives the codes legitimacy beyond a fan riff.

impact.rationale: The 2 million promo-code applications is a concrete, well-anchored Direct-response number — exactly the kind of counted audience action the category rewards. The +44% lift framed as 'the most successful promo code campaign for Uber Eats grocery delivery ever' is unbased — +44% against what comparable baseline? — and the 290,000 earlier figure is presented without clear time framing. No press strip on the board limits the third-party validation.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the horror-quote-as-promo-code is a clean cultural-moment mechanic with 2 million counted code applications, but the headline +44% lift floats without an anchored comparison and the broader strategic stakes sit at seasonal-promo level.
